mnemonics: Decoders Do Demultiplex: Think of a decoder as a demultiplexer with the data input permanently set to 1.
2^n Outputs, n Inputs: A decoder with n inputs will always have 2^n outputs.
Active Low, Look Out!: Remember that many decoder outputs are active-low, meaning they are 'on' when logic 0.
keyPoints: A decoder converts a binary code of n inputs into a one-hot code of 2^n outputs.
Only one output is active (typically high) for any given input combination.
Decoders can be used as fundamental building blocks for implementing combinational logic, like in a Sum-of-Products form.
commonMistakes: Forgetting to account for active-low outputs when connecting to other components, expecting a high signal.
Confusing a decoder with an encoder, which performs the inverse function (2^n inputs to n outputs).
Misunderstanding the enable input's function; an inactive enable will force all outputs to their inactive state, regardless of the input code.
